name: Repeated plasmapheresis did not rejuvenate epigenetic clocks and may have accelerated several measures
summary: Healthy-adult repeated plasmapheresis shifted several biochemical markers but did not produce epigenetic rejuvenation and showed possible acceleration on several epigenetic clock or pace measures.
statement: In healthy plasma donors aged 40 to 60, repeated plasmapheresis changed lipids, proteins, minerals, and blood indices but did not improve epigenetic aging measures; several reported clock or pace measures moved in an unfavorable direction.
population_or_model: Healthy blood donors aged 40 to 60 years
time_horizon: Repeated donations over an approximately 18-week protocol with registry follow-up through study completion
quantitative_note: ClinicalTrials.gov lists 41 actual participants; the paper reports 38 completing the first half and 34 completing the full study.
caveats: This was a plasma-donation/plasmapheresis protocol without young plasma, albumin replacement, or IVIG.
Epigenetic clocks are surrogate biomarkers and should not be treated as clinical outcomes.
The result is limiting evidence for healthy-adult plasmapheresis, not a direct test of AMBAR-style albumin-replacement TPE.
